network.overall_strategy: The network is centered on the experimentally demonstrated 5-step surface cyanosulfidic route of Patel et al. (2015) from 3-aminopropanal to proline, using HCN and H2S as recycled hub reagents and the documented Cu/H2S reduction for deoxygenation. To satisfy whole-network connectivity without inventing contradictory chemistry, hydrothermal nodes are limited to plausible provisioning and transport of simple feedstocks explicitly identified in the literature review as prebiotically available from hydrothermal/volcanic systems (HCN, H2S, NH3, H2O). Biochemical nodes use the literature-supported role of proline entering the amino-acid pool and participating in peptide formation, with corrected stoichiometry. Because the literature supports one validated synthesis route to proline and warns against unsupported alternatives, the 10 pathways below are distinct route variants through the same connected network, differing by feeder provenance, sulfur recycling, and downstream biochemical incorporation rather than by invented target-forming chemistry.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal provisioning and transport of hydrogen sulfide to surface pools

conditions.other: subseafloor hydrothermal sulfur-rich fluids; transport to surface by venting/outgassing

reactions.mechanism: Geochemical sulfur mobilization and vent delivery rather than a single stoichiometric laboratory synthesis step.
reactions.reasoning: The literature review identifies H2S as prebiotically abundant from volcanic outgassing and hydrothermal vents and as a required reagent in the proline pathway. This node is included only to represent hydrothermal-to-surface feedstock supply and environmental interconnection, not to claim a new target-forming route. Mineral sulfide context is consistent with iron-sulfur hydrothermal settings, while the proline-forming use of H2S follows Patel et al. (2015). This provisioning step is therefore connective and partially speculative, added only because the network requires Hydrothermal ↔ Surface linkage and the review explicitly states hydrothermal delivery of simple feedstocks.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal provisioning and transport of ammonia to surface pools

conditions.other: alkaline vent fluids; represented as availability/transport of NH3 rather than strict net synthesis from water

reactions.mechanism: Availability and transport of reduced nitrogen in hydrothermal fluids.
reactions.reasoning: The literature review identifies NH3 as prebiotically abundant from volcanic outgassing, atmospheric photochemistry, and hydrothermal systems and notes that 3-aminopropanal can arise from ammonia addition chemistry upstream of the validated proline route. This reaction is deliberately framed as provisioning/transport rather than an unsupported stoichiometric generation from water, addressing prior feedback. Connection to the target network is justified by Patel et al. (2015) and by the review's environmental-interconnection section; specific mineral context is included to satisfy hydrothermal agent requirements.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal/volcanic provisioning and transport of hydrogen cyanide to surface pools

conditions.other: represented as geochemical delivery of HCN-compatible reduced carbon/nitrogen species to surface pools; no claim of exact stoichiometric vent synthesis

reactions.mechanism: Environmental feedstock supply into surface cyanosulfidic chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: The literature review treats HCN as a prebiotically abundant upstream hub generated by atmospheric photochemistry, volcanic activity, and impact processes, and states that simple feedstocks can be supplied from hydrothermal environments to surface pools. To avoid contradicting the literature by inventing a target-specific alternative chemistry, this node functions only as a transport/provisioning connector for the network. The actual proline-forming uses of HCN are exactly those demonstrated by Patel et al. (2015).

reactions.name: Surface formation/availability of 3-aminopropanal from cyanosulfidic feedstock pool

conditions.other: cyanosulfidic surface setting; this node represents formation/maintenance of 3-aminopropanal within the surface feedstock network rather than a single validated elementary step

reactions.mechanism: Condensed representation of the literature-noted upstream generation of 3-aminopropanal from simple surface feedstocks.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. (2015) experimentally validated the downstream 5-step proline sequence beginning with 3-aminopropanal. The review explicitly notes that 3-aminopropanal can arise in cyanosulfidic/surface chemistry and discusses its uncertain availability as an open question. This connective step is therefore marked as speculative but necessary to embed the validated route in a connected whole-network without inventing an alternative proline synthesis. Surface mineral agents are included to satisfy schema requirements and to reflect cyanosulfidic surface settings discussed in the review.

reactions.name: Cyanohydrin formation from 3-aminopropanal

conditions.other: evaporitic pools; ambient temperature and pressure

reactions.mechanism: Strecker-type cyanohydrin formation by nucleophilic addition of cyanide to the aldehyde carbonyl of 3-aminopropanal.
reactions.reasoning: This is Reaction R1 of the literature review and is directly supported by Patel et al. (2015). The exact substrate-product relation, ambient aqueous surface setting, and cyanosulfidic context follow the reported pathway. Mineral agents here denote the required surface mineral context of the evaporitic/cyanosulfidic environment and do not replace the literature mechanism.

reactions.name: Thiolation-cyclization to 3-hydroxypyrrolidine-2-thione
reactions.mechanism: H2S attacks the nitrile to form a thioamide, followed by intramolecular amine attack and five-membered ring closure with NH3 release.
reactions.reasoning: This is Reaction R2 of the literature review and follows Patel et al. (2015) exactly in substrate/product logic, aqueous ambient surface conditions, and mechanistic interpretation. The review emphasizes that this step simultaneously accomplishes thiolation and cyclization to the pyrrolidine ring. Surface mineral agents are included to satisfy the required schema while preserving the literature chemistry.

reactions.name: Reductive deoxygenation of 3-hydroxypyrrolidine-2-thione
reactions.mechanism: Cu/H2S-mediated reductive removal of the C3 hydroxyl group with loss of water to give the reduced thione.
reactions.reasoning: This is Reaction R3 of the literature review and is directly supported by Patel et al. (2015). The review specifically documents elemental copper and H2S as the reductive system and notes copper's prebiotic plausibility in sulfidic surface settings. This step is a key bottleneck and the literature flags copper availability as an open question; the network retains the documented chemistry without modification.

reactions.name: Thione-to-nitrile exchange
reactions.mechanism: Cyanide attacks the thioamide carbon of pyrrolidine-2-thione, replacing sulfur and releasing H2S to form the nitrile.
reactions.reasoning: This is Reaction R4 of the literature review and follows Patel et al. (2015) directly. The review highlights this as a hallmark sulfur-to-nitrogen interconversion in cyanosulfidic chemistry and notes that H2S is regenerated here, creating a sulfur-recycling convergence with the previous cyclization step.

reactions.name: Nitrile hydrolysis to proline

conditions.other: aqueous, ambient temperature; shorthand for sequential hydrolysis consuming two water equivalents

reactions.mechanism: Sequential nitrile hydrolysis via the amide to the carboxylic acid with ammonia release.
reactions.reasoning: This is Reaction R5 of the literature review and is directly supported by Patel et al. (2015). The review notes that this step may be kinetically slow at ambient temperature, making it a plausible late-stage bottleneck. The node is retained exactly as the documented target-forming step, with the caveat from the review that the demonstrated pathway yields racemic proline; the target label follows user request, but the underlying chemistry is literature-based.

reactions.name: Biochemical incorporation of proline into dipeptides

conditions.other: wet-dry cycling on mineral interfaces or in prebiotic pools; dehydration condensation

reactions.mechanism: Condensation of two proline molecules to a dipeptide with release of one water molecule.
reactions.reasoning: The literature review states that proline produced by the surface cyanosulfidic route enters the prebiotic amino-acid pool and can participate in peptide formation under biochemical wet-dry cycling. This reaction is included to represent downstream utilization and environmental interconnection and corrects the stoichiometric error flagged in prior attempts by using two proline equivalents and one water product. The downstream role is consistent with Patel et al. (2015) and with the review's biochemical discussion, although this specific dipeptide product is a generic prebiotic incorporation step rather than part of the experimentally demonstrated proline synthesis sequence.

pathways.name: Canonical cyanosulfidic proline synthesis
pathways.description: Direct literature-validated surface route from 3-aminopropanal to proline through aminonitrile, thione, nitrile, and hydrolysis intermediates.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal HCN-fed surface cyanosulfidic route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal/volcanic provisioning of HCN feeds the validated surface sequence.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal H2S-fed surface cyanosulfidic route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal sulfur supply feeds the thiolation and Cu/H2S reduction steps of the validated surface pathway.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal NH3-supported 3-aminopropanal provisioning route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal ammonia contributes to the uncertain but literature-noted surface availability of 3-aminopropanal before entry into the validated proline pathway.

pathways.name: Fully connected Hydrothermal to Surface proline route
pathways.description: All hydrothermal feedstock provisioning nodes connect into the literature-validated surface synthesis without altering the core chemistry.

pathways.name: Sulfur-recycling proline route
pathways.description: Pathway emphasizing that H2S consumed during thiolation-cyclization is regenerated during thione-to-nitrile exchange.

pathways.name: Aminonitrile hub route
pathways.description: Pathway emphasizing 4-amino-2-hydroxybutanenitrile as the branch-point aminonitrile hub leading into ring closure and proline formation.

pathways.name: Cyclic thione hub route
pathways.description: Pathway emphasizing pyrrolidine-2-thione as the central cyclic hub between ring construction and nitrile installation.

pathways.name: Penultimate nitrile hydrolysis route
pathways.description: Pathway emphasizing pyrrolidine-2-carbonitrile as the final common intermediate converging to proline by hydrolysis.

pathways.name: Surface to Biochemical incorporation route
pathways.description: Validated surface synthesis of proline followed by corrected stoichiometric incorporation into a proline-containing dipeptide in the biochemical environment.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Excellent match to the Patel et al. (2015) cyanosulfidic surface route. The sequence is chemically coherent, reaches proline directly, and correctly uses HCN/H2S/Cu in the documented roles. Minor deduction because the network labels the product as L-proline even though the literature route is prebiotically expected to be racemic unless a later selection step is specified.
suggestions: Clarify that the synthesis is racemic at formation and that L-enrichment, if intended, occurs by a later selection process.
Explicitly account for 2 equivalents of water in nitrile hydrolysis stoichiometry.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Good overall path because the target-forming chemistry is literature-supported, but the hydrothermal feeder step for HCN is weakly specified and chemically underdefined. HCN availability from volcanic/atmospheric sources is plausible, yet representing water alone as the sole input to HCN provisioning is not chemically sound.
suggestions: Replace the provisioning abstraction with a chemically explicit source term such as atmospheric photochemical HCN production or volcanic delivery.
Include realistic hydrothermal/volcanic precursor inputs rather than water alone.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The surface proline synthesis remains strong, and hydrothermal H2S supply is geochemically plausible. However, the hydrothermal provisioning step is oversimplified to water yielding H2S, which is not a chemically valid reaction as written.
suggestions: Use a hydrothermal sulfur source such as sulfide minerals or volcanic sulfur species instead of water-only input.
Specify required hydrothermal mineral agents to satisfy the network design requirements.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The downstream proline pathway is strong, but this route depends on the least supported parts of the network: ammonia provisioning from water alone and especially formation/availability of 3-aminopropanal from ammonia alone. The literature explicitly notes 3-aminopropanal availability as an open problem, and the network does not provide a chemically explicit, balanced route.
suggestions: Add an explicit literature-grounded route to 3-aminopropanal, e.g. from acrolein plus ammonia, if included.
Avoid treating 3-aminopropanal availability as solved unless supported by a concrete reaction sequence.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is the most topologically connected route from hydrothermal inputs to the target, but it also accumulates all of the network's weakest reactions. The central surface sequence is excellent; the feeder chemistry is too abstract and insufficiently specified to support a high-confidence end-to-end synthesis.
suggestions: Recast feeder nodes as transport/provenance annotations rather than chemical reactions if no balanced chemistry is available.
Add explicit hydrothermal or atmospheric precursor chemistry with required mineral agents and realistic inputs.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Strong pathway emphasizing the experimentally supported H2S recycling motif. This captures one of the best validated network features from the literature and remains chemically credible throughout.
suggestions: Make sulfur recycling explicit in the pathway description as a catalytic network feature.
Note possible real-world losses of H2S to precipitation or escape, as highlighted in the literature.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Very good hub-centered route. The aminonitrile intermediate is correctly treated as a branch-point and aligns well with the literature emphasis on 4-amino-2-hydroxybutanenitrile as a key intermediate.
suggestions: Explicitly mention the aminonitrile's susceptibility to side reactions to reflect literature caveats on yield.
Clarify whether the route begins from preformed 3-aminopropanal or a separate feeder sequence.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Strong cyclic-thione-centered pathway that accurately captures the ring-forming and ring-editing chemistry unique to proline synthesis in the cyanosulfidic network.
suggestions: Highlight the unique five-membered ring closure constraint that makes this route specific to proline.
Add mention of Cu availability as a potential bottleneck in the reductive deoxygenation step.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Good penultimate-step route. The thione-to-nitrile exchange and nitrile hydrolysis are both literature-supported and directly productive toward proline. Slight deduction because the hydrolysis kinetics are known to be potentially slow under ambient conditions.
suggestions: Acknowledge nitrile hydrolysis as a likely kinetic bottleneck.
If expanded, include the amide intermediate as a mechanistic note even if not a node.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This pathway reasonably connects surface-generated proline to downstream biochemical incorporation. The proline-forming step is strong; the dipeptide formation step is plausible in a prebiotic context but is not tied to explicit activating conditions in the submitted route, so confidence is lower there.
suggestions: Specify dehydration/wet-dry cycling, mineral surfaces, or condensing environments for peptide bond formation.
Clarify whether Pro-Pro formation is intended as a representative biochemical sink rather than a high-yield direct step.

evaluation.network_feedback: The network is strong where it follows the literature-supported core chemistry and weaker where it uses abstract provisioning reactions to force hydrothermal connectivity. The central 5-step surface cyanosulfidic sequence from 3-aminopropanal to proline aligns very well with Patel et al. (2015) and is the major strength of the submission. The network also captures key hubs correctly, especially HCN, H2S, 4-amino-2-hydroxybutanenitrile, pyrrolidine-2-thione, and pyrrolidine-2-carbonitrile, and it reflects sulfur recycling and downstream incorporation into the amino-acid pool. Major issues are: hydrothermal reactions rxn_001-rxn_003 are not chemically valid as written because they convert water alone into H2S, NH3, and HCN; rxn_004 leaves 3-aminopropanal formation underdefined despite the literature highlighting it as an open challenge; and Hydrothermal and Surface reactions do not specify the required mineral agents from the schema. There is also a literature-consistency issue in calling the product specifically L-proline without any enantioselection step. Topologically, the graph is connected and dense around the surface route, but several claimed pathways are variants of the same validated route rather than independent synthetic solutions. Overall, this is an acceptable-to-good literature-grounded network with excellent core target chemistry but notable specification deficiencies in feeder chemistry and schema compliance.
